narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your background includes a middle-income family, part-time enrollment plans, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 31, lower than the usual 36.70. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 74.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. Most significantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—pushed the model firmly toward expecting bar failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong academic performance. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the typical 5.7 average, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often predict ongoing difficulties. Finally, opting for part-time status, which is uncommon compared to the 93% of students who go full-time, added further weight toward the failure prediction, likely because it correlates with divided focus in past cases. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to rank in the middle or higher deciles and pursue full-time study, your metrics placed you in a riskier group for bar success. While other factors like your LSAT and GPA played roles, these top three were the decisive drivers steering the model to its conclusion. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from real student outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your recent grades to improve those decile rankings, or exploring full-time options if feasible. Building a stronger academic trajectory now could shift future predictions in your favor, and reaching out to admissions advisors for targeted advice might uncover pathways like conditional acceptance programs. You're taking a thoughtful step by understanding this feedback, and with focused improvements, law school doors can reopen.
